planting.note: Sow directly after last frost once soil is warm; successive sowings every 3-4 weeks extend the harvest window through summer.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1/2 inch deep in full sun after the last frost date, thinning seedlings to 6-12 inches apart. Unlike root radishes, allow plants to bolt and flower — the long, curved seed pods are the edible crop, best harvested at 3-6 inches before they become tough and fibrous. Pick pods frequently to encourage continued production; plants in active harvest can produce prolifically for several weeks. Eat pods raw in salads, stir-fry, or quick-pickle in rice vinegar for an excellent condiment.

planting.note: Sow in early spring directly into sandy, free-draining soil; tolerates saline conditions and coastal exposure where most vegetables struggle.

directions: Sow seeds shallowly, about 1/4 inch deep, directly into sandy or well-drained soil in a sunny position in early spring. Sea rocket is exceptionally tolerant of salt spray, coastal winds, and drought once established, requiring very little supplemental water. Harvest young, succulent leaves and stems regularly for use raw in salads where they contribute a pleasant peppery bite; young seed pods are also edible and can be added to salads or pickled. Allow some plants to set seed for reliable self-seeding in subsequent years.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to early autumn for a winter and early spring harvest; plants overwinter as small rosettes in mild climates and are among the first greens ready in spring.

directions: Sow seeds thinly, 1/4 inch deep, in late summer or early autumn into fertile, moisture-retentive soil, thinning to 4-6 inches apart. Germination is most reliable when soil temperatures have dropped below 65°F; cool nights after late-August sowings are ideal. Harvest entire rosettes by cutting at the base once they reach a few inches across, or pick outer leaves continuously throughout winter. Keeled corn salad has a mild, slightly sweet flavor well suited to winter salads alongside mâche and other cold-hardy greens.

planting.note: In zones 8-10, sow in autumn for winter and spring harvests; in cooler zones, sow in early spring as temperatures warm above 55°F.

directions: Sow seeds 1/4 inch deep in full sun into well-drained, moderately fertile soil; plants thrive in the same rocky or sandy conditions that challenge most vegetables. Once established, reichardia is remarkably drought-tolerant and requires minimal irrigation, making it an excellent choice for dry summer gardens in the Mediterranean climate zone. Harvest basal leaves as needed throughout the cooler months, cutting back to encourage fresh, tender regrowth; the plant is perennial in frost-free zones and will produce for several years from a single sowing. Young leaves are mild enough for raw salads, while older leaves are better cooked briefly as a spinach substitute.

planting.note: Sow in early spring, 4-2 weeks before the last frost; nipplewort is cold-hardy and germinates readily in cool soil under dappled light.

directions: Scatter seeds shallowly on the soil surface or cover with no more than 1/8 inch of soil in a partially shaded spot in early spring; nipplewort is one of the few edible annuals that performs well in the shade of hedges or woodland edges. Thin or transplant seedlings to 6-12 inches apart and keep soil evenly moist through cool weather. Harvest the young, tender basal leaves and shoot tips before the plant bolts, cooking them briefly like spinach to reduce their mild bitterness. Allow a few plants to self-seed at the end of the season; nipplewort will reliably return each year with no further effort.

planting.note: Direct sow 4-2 weeks before the last frost; the tiny seeds germinate quickly in cool soil and rosettes develop fast in early spring light.

directions: Surface-sow or barely cover seeds in full sun in early spring, thinning to 6-10 inches once seedlings are established; smooth hawk's beard germinates quickly and is ready to harvest within a month of sowing. Cut young rosette leaves when they are 3-4 inches long for the mildest flavor, using them raw in salads or wilting briefly in a pan with olive oil and garlic. Plants can be cut back to within an inch of the crown and will regrow for additional harvests before bolting triggers the small yellow flower heads. Self-seeding is prolific in disturbed ground, making smooth hawk's beard a low-maintenance, cut-and-come-again addition to the productive garden.
